rows.v: Chuck Roast, Flat Iron Steak, Denver Steak, Blade Steak
Low-and-slow braising and pot roasts — collagen melts into rich gelatin over hours.
The chuck primal contains some of the most well-exercised muscles on the cow, which is why cuts like chuck roast are so flavorful — but also why they need long, slow cooking to become tender.
Ribeye Steak, Prime Rib (Standing Rib Roast), Back Ribs
High-heat grilling or roasting — exceptional marbling bastes the meat as it cooks.
The rib primal supplies only about 10 ribs total per side, and the prized ribeye comes from this small section — explaining why it commands such a premium price.
Tenderloin (Filet Mignon), New York Strip, T-Bone, Porterhouse
Quick, high-heat searing to medium-rare — the most tender cuts on the animal.
The tenderloin muscle does so little work in a living cow that it accounts for less than 2% of the animal's total weight, yet it's often the most expensive cut at the butcher counter.
Sirloin Steak, Tri-Tip
Marinate, then grill or reverse-sear — leaner cuts that reward a flavor boost.
Tri-tip was largely unknown outside of California until the 1950s, when a Santa Maria butcher began selling it as a steak instead of grinding it into hamburger.
Top Round, Bottom Round, Eye of Round, Rump Roast
Roast to medium-rare and slice thin, or braise for tenderness — the leanest primal.
Because the round primal does so much work supporting the animal's weight, its muscles are some of the leanest on the cow — making them popular for health-conscious cuts like London broil.
Whole Brisket, Flat Cut, Point Cut
Low-and-slow smoking for 10+ hours — collagen breaks down into tender, sliceable meat.
A whole brisket can take 10-14 hours to smoke properly, and during that time it passes through a tough 'stall' phase before the collagen finally breaks down into tender gelatin.
Skirt Steak, Hanger Steak, Short Ribs
Marinate and grill hot and fast, or braise low and slow — always slice against the grain.
Hanger steak gets its name because it 'hangs' from the diaphragm, and there's only one per animal — for years, butchers kept it for themselves rather than selling it.
Flank Steak
Marinate, grill hot and fast to medium-rare, slice thin against the grain.
Flank steak's muscle fibers run in one direction the entire length of the cut, which is why slicing against the grain makes such a dramatic difference in tenderness.
Soup Bones, Osso Buco, Stew Meat
Long, moist cooking — simmering or braising turns collagen into rich gelatin and stock.
The shank is so rich in collagen that simmering it for hours is one of the oldest methods humans have used to make gelatin — the same substance found in gummy candy.
